140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
347	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
349	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
350	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
351	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
352	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
353	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
354	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
355	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
356	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
357	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
358	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
359	  %  function
360	  %  $$
361	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
362	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
363	  %  $$
364	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
365	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
366	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
367	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
368	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
369	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
370	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
371	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
372	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
373	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
374	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
375	  such that for all \(n > N\)
376	  \[
377	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
378	  \]
379	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
380	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
381	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
382	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
383	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
384	
385	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
386	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
387	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
388	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
389	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
390	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
391	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
392	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
393	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
394	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
395	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
396	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
397	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
398	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
399	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
400	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
404	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
405	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality infrastructure (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is
406	  %   formalized and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration, but
407	  %   it is no longer blocked on a Mathlib gap: the ambient subquotient route
408	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps the missing graded-quotient /
409	  %   graded-kernel / regrading API by keeping every object an ambient family
410	  %   `fun n => Naux ⊓ ℳ n` of submodules of the fixed `M`. No `DirectSum.Decomposition`
411	  %   on a subtype `↥p` or quotient `M ⧸ p` carrier is ever constructed, so the
412	  %   `DirectSum.IsInternal` / `Submodule.map_iSup` `isDefEq`/`whnf` runaway cannot fire.
413	  %   The residual is the ambient bookkeeping build of the subquotient blocks below.
414	\end{lemma}
415	
416	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
417	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
418	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
419	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
420	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
421	%  following short exact sequence
422	%  $$
423	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
424	%  $$
425	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
426	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
427	\begin{proof}
428	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
429	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
430	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
431	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
432	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
433	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
434	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
435	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
436	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
437	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
438	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
439	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
440	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
441	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
442	
443	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
444	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
445	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
446	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
447	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
448	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
449	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
450	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
451	  is the number on which we induct.
452	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
453	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
454	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
455	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
456	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
457	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
458	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
459	
460	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
461	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
462	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
463	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
464	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
465	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
466	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
467	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
468	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
469	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
470	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
471	
472	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
473	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
474	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
475	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
476	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
477	  \[
478	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
479	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
480	  \]
481	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
482	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
483	  with
484	  \[
485	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
486	    \qquad (n > N),
487	  \]
488	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
489	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
490	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
491	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
492	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
493	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
494	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
495	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
496	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
497	\end{proof}
498	
499	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
500	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
501	% NOTE: The Lean declarations pinned in this subsection
502	% (coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, rationalHilbert_antidiff, IsRatHilb,
503	% IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero, .bump, .sub, .shiftRight, .antidiff, .ofDiffEq) are
504	% currently `private` in the Lean source, so their public-name \lean{} pins may not
505	% resolve until the IsRatHilb toolkit is split out into its own module
506	% (GradedHilbertSerre.lean) and the helpers are de-private. The mathematical content
507	% of the blocks is unaffected.
508	
509	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
510	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
511	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
512	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
513	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
514	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
515	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
516	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
517	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
518	
519	\begin{lemma}\leanok
520	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
521	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
523	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
524	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
525	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
526	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
527	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
528	  \(F\):
529	  \[
530	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
531	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
532	  \]
533	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
534	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
535	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
536	  the Cauchy product.
537	\end{lemma}
538	
539	\begin{lemma}\leanok
540	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
541	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
543	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
544	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
545	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
546	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
547	  \[
548	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
549	  \]
550	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
551	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
552	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
553	  \[
554	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
555	  \]
556	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
557	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
558	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
559	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
560	\end{lemma}
561	
562	\begin{proof}
563	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
564	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
565	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
566	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
567	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
568	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
569	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
570	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
571	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
572	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
573	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
574	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
575	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
576	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
577	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
578	\end{proof}
579	
580	\begin{definition}\leanok
581	[Rational Hilbert function]
582	  \label{def:ratHilb}
583	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
584	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
585	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
586	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
587	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
588	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
589	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
590	  \[
591	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
592	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
593	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
594	  \]
595	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
596	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
597	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
598	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
599	\end{definition}
600	
601	\begin{lemma}\leanok
602	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
603	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
604	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
605	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
606	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
607	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
608	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
609	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
610	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
611	\end{lemma}
612	
613	\begin{lemma}\leanok
614	[Raising the pole order]
615	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
617	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
618	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
619	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
620	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
621	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
622	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
623	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
624	\end{lemma}
625	
626	\begin{lemma}\leanok
627	[Closure under difference]
628	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
629	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
630	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
631	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
632	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
633	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
634	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
635	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
636	\end{lemma}
637	
638	\begin{lemma}\leanok
639	[Closure under right shift]
640	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
641	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
642	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
643	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
644	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
645	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
646	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
647	  Lean.
648	\end{lemma}
649	
650	\begin{lemma}\leanok
651	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
652	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
653	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
654	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
655	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
656	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
657	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
658	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
659	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
660	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
661	\end{lemma}
662	
663	\begin{proof}
664	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
665	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
666	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
667	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
668	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
669	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
670	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
671	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
672	\end{proof}
673	
674	\begin{lemma}\leanok
675	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
676	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
677	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
678	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
679	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
680	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
681	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
682	  \[
683	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
684	  \]
685	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
686	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
687	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
688	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
689	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
690	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
691	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
692	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
693	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
694	\end{lemma}
695	
696	\begin{proof}
697	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
698	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
699	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
700	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
701	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
702	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
703	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
704	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
705	\end{proof}
706	
707	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
708	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
710	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
711	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
712	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
713	  % Polynomials" (read from
714	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
715	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
716	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
717	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
718	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
719	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
720	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
721	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
722	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
723	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
724	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
725	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
726	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
727	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
728	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
729	  \[
730	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
731	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
732	  \]
733	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
734	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
735	\end{theorem}
736	
737	\begin{proof}
738	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
739	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
740	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
741	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
742	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
743	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
744	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
745	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
746	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
747	  function
748	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
749	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
750	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
751	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
752	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
753	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
754	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
755	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
756	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
757	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
758	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
759	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
760	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
761	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
762	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
763	\end{proof}
764	
765	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
766	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
767	
768	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
769	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
770	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
771	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
772	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
773	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
774	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
775	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
776	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
777	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
778	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
779	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
780	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
781	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
782	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
783	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
784	
785	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
786	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
787	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
788	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
789	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
790	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
791	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
792	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
793	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
794	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
795	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
796	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
797	
798	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
799	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
800	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
801	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
802	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
803	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
804	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
805	\(x \in R_1\).
806	
807	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
808	
809	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
810	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
811	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
812	  \mathlibok
813	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
814	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
815	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
816	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
817	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
818	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
819	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
820	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
821	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
822	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
823	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
824	\end{lemma}
825	
826	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
827	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
828	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
829	  \mathlibok
830	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
831	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
832	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
833	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
834	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
835	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
836	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
837	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
838	\end{lemma}
839	
840	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
841	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
842	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
843	  \mathlibok
844	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
845	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
846	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
847	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
848	  \[
849	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
850	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
851	  \]
852	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
853	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
854	\end{lemma}
855	
856	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
857	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
858	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
859	  \mathlibok
860	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
861	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
862	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
863	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
864	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
865	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two
866	  subquotients, this transfers their finite generation down one generator
867	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
868	\end{lemma}
869	
870	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
871	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
872	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
873	  \mathlibok
874	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
875	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
876	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
877	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
878	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
879	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
880	  underlies the ambient identity \(x \cdot N \cap M_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap M_n)\)
881	  used in the subquotient closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and
882	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}).
883	\end{lemma}
884	
885	\begin{lemma}[Commutative subalgebra generated by commuting elements]
886	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
887	  \lean{Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin}
888	  \mathlibok
889	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
890	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Subalgebra.Lattice}).}
891	  Let \(R\) be a commutative semiring and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra, possibly
892	  noncommutative. If \(s \subseteq A\) is a set of pairwise-commuting elements
893	  (\(a b = b a\) for all \(a, b \in s\)), then the \(R\)-subalgebra
894	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_R(s)\) generated by \(s\) has commutative multiplication; the
895	  commutative-ring structure on it follows. Applied to the \(r\) pairwise-commuting degree-raising endomorphisms
896	  \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1} \in \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\), it yields a
897	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots,
898	  x_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); the polynomial action of
899	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) on \(M\) factors through this commutative \(A\)
900	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
901	\end{lemma}

903	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
904	
905	\begin{lemma}\leanok
906	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
907	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
908	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
909	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
910	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
911	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
912	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
913	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
914	  \(n\),
915	  \[
916	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
917	  \]
918	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
919	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
920	\end{lemma}
921	
922	\begin{proof}
923	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
924	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
925	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
926	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
927	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
928	\end{proof}
929	
930	\begin{lemma}\leanok
931	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
932	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
933	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
934	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
935	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
936	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
937	  \[
938	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
939	  \]
940	  Together with the independence of
941	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
942	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
943	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
944	\end{lemma}
945	
946	\begin{proof}
947	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
948	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
949	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
950	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
951	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
952	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
953	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
954	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
955	\end{proof}

1171	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), with \(\mathcal{F}'\) the tilde of
1172	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
1173	\end{proof}

1229	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1230	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1231	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1232	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1233	
1234	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1235	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1236	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1237	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1238	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1239	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1240	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1241	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1242	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1243	\end{lemma}
1244	\begin{proof}
1245	
1246	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1247	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1248	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1249	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1250	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1251	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1252	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1253	\end{proof}
1254	
1255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1256	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1257	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1259	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1260	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1261	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1262	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1263	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1264	\end{lemma}
1265	\begin{proof}
1266	
1267	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1268	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1269	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1270	\end{proof}

1273	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the
1274	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---

1276	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1277	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1278	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1280	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1281	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1282	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1283	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1284	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1285	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1286	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1287	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1288	  % L933--938).
1289	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1290	  % equality
1291	  % $$
1292	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1293	  % $$
1294	  % as $R'$-modules."
1295	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1296	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1297	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1298	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1299	  \[
1300	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1301	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1302	  \]
1303	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1304	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1305	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1306	  hypothesis.
1307	\end{lemma}
1308	
1309	\begin{proof}
1310	  \uses{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv, lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1311	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1312	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the standalone geometry-free helper
1313	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}, whose construction is now the
1314	  natively \(R'\)-linear pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1315	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1316	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1317	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1318	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1319	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1320	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1321	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1322	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1323	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1324	
1325	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1326	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1327	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1328	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1329	  dictionaries,
1330	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1331	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1332	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1333	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1334	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1335	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1336	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1337	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1338	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1339	  \[
1340	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1341	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1342	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1343	  \]
1344	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1345	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1346	
1347	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1348	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1349	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1350	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1351	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1352	\end{proof}

1526	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1527	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1528	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1529	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1530	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1531	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1532	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1533	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1534	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1535	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N

1537	  the stated equation.

1541	% These three project-bespoke atoms record that, on global sections over Spec R, the two

1546	\begin{lemma}

1550	  \label{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}

1552	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
1553	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to \operatorname{Spec} R\) be composable morphisms of
1554	  schemes and \(M\) an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_1}\)-module. Applying the global-sections functor
1555	  \(\Gamma\) (over the base \(R\)) to the component
1556	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\) of the pushforward-composition coherence
1557	  isomorphism yields the identity morphism:
1558	  \[
1559	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_M\bigr) = \operatorname{id}.
1560	  \]
1561	  The pushforward-composition coherence is transparent at the level of sections.

1618	\begin{lemma}
1619	[(iii-1) Unit expansion: inverting the comp-coherence]
1620	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand}
1621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand}
1622	  \uses{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1623	  Let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the canonical
1624	  isomorphism, \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) the left tensor inclusion, and set
1625	  \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\). For an \(A\)-module \(M\), the bare \((g')\)-unit
1626	  \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\) of the \((g')\)-adjunction expands as the four-factor composite
1627	  \[
1628	    \eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}
1629	      \;=\;
1630	      \eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}
1631	      \,\circ\, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\bigl(\eta^{e}_{(\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^{*}\widetilde M}\bigr)
1632	      \,\circ\, (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{\widetilde M}
1633	      \,\circ\, (g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{\widetilde M}\bigr),
1634	  \]
1635	  where composites are read left to right.
1636	\end{lemma}
1637	\begin{proof}
1638	  \uses{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1639	  The leg-reindex engine Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
1640	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), reads
1641	  \[
1642	    \eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}
1643	      \circ (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}(\eta^{e})
1644	      \circ (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}
1645	    \;=\;
1646	    \eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}
1647	      \circ (g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{inv}}\bigr).
1648	  \]
1649	  Post-compose both sides with \((g')_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}\bigr)\).
1650	  Since \((g')_{*}\) is a functor it carries
1651	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp})^{\mathrm{inv}} \circ (\mathrm{pullbackComp})^{\mathrm{hom}} = \mathrm{id}\)
1652	  to the identity, so the right-hand side collapses to \(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\), giving the stated
1653	  expansion.
1654	\end{proof}

1880	      \(e\)-pieces inside the codomain read

880	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
881	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
882	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).

885	\begin{proof}
886	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
887	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
888	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
889	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
890	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
891	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
892	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
893	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
894	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
895	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
896	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
897	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
898	\end{proof}

957	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
958	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
959	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
960	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
961	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
962	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
963	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
964	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
965	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
966	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
967	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).

1012	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1013	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1014	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1015	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1016	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1017	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1018	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1019	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
1020	\end{lemma}
1021	
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1024	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1025	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1026	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1027	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1028	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1029	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1030	
1031	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1032	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1033	
1034	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1035	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1036	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1037	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1038	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1039	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1040	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1041	
1042	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1043	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1044	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1045	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1046	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1047	  \(A_g\).
1048	
1049	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1050	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1051	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1052	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1053	
1054	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1055	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1056	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1057	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1058	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1059	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1060	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1061	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1062	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1063	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1064	
1065	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1066	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1067	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1068	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1069	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1070	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1071	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1072	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1073	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1074	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1075	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1076	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1077	
1078	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1079	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1080	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1081	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1082	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1083	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1084	
1085	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1086	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1087	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1088	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1089	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1090	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1091	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1092	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1093	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1094	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1095	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1096	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1097	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1098	
1099	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1100	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1101	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1102	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1103	\end{proof}

1105	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1106	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1107	
1108	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1109	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1110	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1111	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1112	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1113	
1114	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1115	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1117	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1119	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1120	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1121	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1122	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1123	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1124	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1125	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1126	\end{lemma}
1127	\begin{proof}
1128	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1129	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1130	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1131	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1132	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1133	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1134	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1135	\end{proof}

1137	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1138	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1139	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1140	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1141	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1142	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1143	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1144	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1145	\end{lemma}
1146	\begin{proof}
1147	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1148	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1149	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1150	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1151	\end{proof}

1153	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1154	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1156	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1157	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1158	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1159	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1160	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1161	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1162	\end{lemma}
1163	\begin{proof}
1164	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1165	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1166	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1167	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1168	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1169	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1170	  these conditions gives the claim.
1171	\end{proof}

1173	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1174	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1175	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1176	  \mathlibok
1177	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1178	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1179	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1180	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1181	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1182	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1183	\end{lemma}

1185	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1186	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1187	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1188	  \mathlibok
1189	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1190	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1191	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1192	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).

1196	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1197	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1199	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1200	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1201	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1202	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1203	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1204	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1205	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1206	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1207	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1208	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1209	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1210	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1211	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1212	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1213	  % NOTE (iter-016): the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1214	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1215	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1216	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1217	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1218	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1219	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1220	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1221	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1222	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1223	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1224	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1225	\end{lemma}
1226	
1227	\begin{proof}
1228	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1229	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1230	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1231	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1232	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1233	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1234	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1235	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1236	
1237	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1238	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1239	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1240	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1241	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1242	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1243	
1244	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1245	  \[
1246	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1247	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1248	  \]
1249	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1250	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1251	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1252	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1253	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1254	
1255	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1256	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1257	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1258	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1259	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1260	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1261	\end{proof}
