1263	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1264	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1266	  % NOTE (iter-008 signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1267	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1268	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1269	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1270	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1271	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1272	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1273	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1274	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1275	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1276	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1277	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1278	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1279	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1280	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1281	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1282	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1283	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1284	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1285	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1286	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1288	  core of the induction).}
1289	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1290	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1291	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1292	
1293	\end{lemma}
1294	
1295	\begin{proof}
1296	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1297	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1298	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1299	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1300	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1301	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE (iter-007): the induction must generalise the base domain A
1302	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1303	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1304	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1305	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1306	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1307	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1308	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1309	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1310	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1311	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1312	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1313	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1314	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1315	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1316	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1317	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1318	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1319	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1320	
1321	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1322	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1323	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1324	
1325	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1326	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1327	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1328	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1329	  \begin{enumerate}
1330	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1331	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1332	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1333	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1334	      \(P_d\)-modules
1335	      \[
1336	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1337	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1338	      \]
1339	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1340	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1341	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1342	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1343	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1344	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1345	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1346	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1347	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1348	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1349	      to \(A_g\).
1350	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1351	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1352	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1353	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1354	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1355	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1356	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1357	      over \(A_f\).
1358	      % NOTE (iter-016): the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1359	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1360	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1361	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1362	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1363	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1364	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1365	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1366	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1367	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1368	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1369	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1370	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1371	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1372	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1373	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1374	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1375	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1376	      completes the induction.
1377	  \end{enumerate}
1378	\end{proof}

1447	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1448	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1449	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1450	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1451	
1452	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1453	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1454	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1455	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1456	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1457	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1458	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1459	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1460	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1461	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1462	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1463	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1464	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1465	
1466	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1467	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1468	\end{theorem}
1469	
1470	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1471	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1472	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1473	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1474	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1475	% by restricting to a non-empty
1476	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1477	\begin{proof}
1478	  
1479	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1480	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1481	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1482	
1483	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1484	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1485	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1486	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1487	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1488	
1489	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1490	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1491	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1492	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1493	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1494	
1495	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1496	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1497	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1498	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1499	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1500	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1501	
1502	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1503	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1504	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1505	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1506	\end{proof}
1507	
1508	\section{Mathlib status}
1509	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1510	
1511	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1512	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1513	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1514	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1515	generic-flatness argument consumes
1516	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1517	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1518	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1519	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1520	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1521	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1522	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1523	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1524	are introduced in
1525	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

958	
959	\begin{definition}\leanok
960	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
961	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
962	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
963	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
964	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
965	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
966	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
967	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
968	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
969	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
970	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
971	  \begin{itemize}
972	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
973	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
974	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
975	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
976	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
977	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
978	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
979	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
980	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
981	  \end{itemize}
982	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
983	  Hilbert function} is
984	  \[
985	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
986	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
987	  \]
988	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
989	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
990	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
991	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
992	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
993	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
994	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
995	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
996	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
997	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
998	\end{definition}
999	
1000	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1001	
1002	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1003	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1004	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1005	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1006	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1007	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1008	
1009	\begin{definition}\leanok
1010	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1011	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1012	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1013	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1014	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1015	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1016	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1017	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1018	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1019	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1020	\end{definition}
1021	
1022	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1023	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1024	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1025	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1026	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1027	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1028	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1029	\end{lemma}
1030	
1031	\begin{proof}
1032	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1033	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1034	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1035	\end{proof}
1036	
1037	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1038	[Degree-shift commutation]
1039	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1041	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1042	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1043	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1044	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1045	  \[
1046	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1047	  \]
1048	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1049	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1050	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1051	\end{lemma}
1052	
1053	\begin{proof}
1054	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1055	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1056	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1057	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1058	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1059	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1060	\end{proof}
1061	
1062	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1063	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1064	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1065	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1066	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1067	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1068	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1069	\end{lemma}
1070	
1071	\begin{proof}
1072	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1073	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1074	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1075	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1076	\end{proof}
1077	
1078	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1079	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1080	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1081	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1082	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1083	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1084	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1085	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1086	\end{lemma}
1087	
1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1091	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1092	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1093	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1094	  homogeneous.
1095	\end{proof}
1096	
1097	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1098	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1099	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1101	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1102	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1103	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1104	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1105	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1106	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1107	\end{lemma}
1108	
1109	\begin{proof}
1110	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1111	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1112	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1113	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1114	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1115	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1116	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1117	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1118	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1119	  homogeneous.
1120	\end{proof}
1121	
1122	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1123	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1124	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1125	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1126	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1127	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1128	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1129	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1130	  this is recorded here.
1131	\end{lemma}
1132	
1133	\begin{proof}
1134	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1135	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1136	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1137	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1138	\end{proof}
1139	
1140	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1141	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1142	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1144	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1145	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1146	\end{lemma}
1147	
1148	\begin{proof}
1149	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1150	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1151	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1152	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1153	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1154	\end{proof}
1155	
1156	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1157	[Ambient image identity]
1158	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1160	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1161	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1162	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1163	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1164	  \[
1165	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1166	  \]
1167	\end{lemma}
1168	
1169	\begin{proof}
1170	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1171	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1172	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1173	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1174	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1175	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1176	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1177	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1178	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1179	\end{proof}
1180	
1181	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1182	[Ambient distributive law]
1183	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1184	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1185	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1186	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1187	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1188	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1189	  \[
1190	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1191	  \]
1192	\end{lemma}
1193	
1194	\begin{proof}
1195	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1196	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1197	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1198	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1199	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1200	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1201	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1202	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1203	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1204	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1205	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1206	\end{proof}

1208	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1209	
1210	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1211	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1212	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1213	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1214	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1215	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1216	
1217	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1218	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1219	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1221	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1222	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1223	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1224	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1225	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1226	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1227	\end{lemma}
1228	
1229	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1230	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1231	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1232	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1233	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1234	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1235	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1236	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1237	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1238	\end{lemma}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[The kernel pair nests]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1244	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1245	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1246	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1247	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1248	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1249	\end{lemma}
1250	
1251	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1252	[The cokernel pair nests]
1253	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1255	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1256	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1257	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1258	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1259	\end{lemma}
1260	
1261	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1262	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1263	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1264	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1265	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1266	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1267	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1268	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1269	\end{lemma}
1270	
1271	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1272	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1273	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1275	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1276	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1277	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1278	\end{lemma}
1279	
1280	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1281	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1282	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1284	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1285	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1286	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1287	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1288	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1292	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1293	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1295	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1296	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1297	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1298	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1299	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1300	\end{lemma}
1301	
1302	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1303	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1304	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1305	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1306	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1307	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1308	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1309	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1310	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1311	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1312	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1313	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1314	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1315	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1316	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1317	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1318	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1319	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1320	  \[
1321	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1322	    \qquad
1323	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1324	  \]
1325	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1326	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1327	  \begin{enumerate}
1328	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1329	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1330	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1331	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1332	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1333	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1334	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1335	  \end{enumerate}
1336	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1337	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1338	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1339	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1340	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1341	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1342	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1343	\end{lemma}
1344	
1345	\begin{proof}
1346	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1347	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1348	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1349	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1350	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1351	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1352	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1353	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1354	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1355	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1356	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1357	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1358	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1359	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1360	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1361	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1362	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1363	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1364	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1365	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1366	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1367	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1368	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1369	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1370	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1371	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1372	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1373	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1374	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1375	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1376	
1377	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1378	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1379	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1380	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1381	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1382	
1383	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1384	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1385	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1386	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1387	
1388	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1389	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1390	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1391	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1392	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1393	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1394	\end{proof}
1395	
1396	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1397	
1398	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1399	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1400	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1402	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1403	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1404	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1405	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1406	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1407	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1408	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1409	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1410	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1411	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1412	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1413	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1414	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1415	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1416	  \[
1417	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1418	  \]
1419	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1420	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1421	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1422	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1423	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1424	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1425	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1426	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1427	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1428	\end{lemma}
1429	
1430	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1431	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1432	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1433	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1434	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1435	%  following short exact sequence
1436	%  $$
1437	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1438	%  $$
1439	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1440	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1441	\begin{proof}
1442	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1443	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1444	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1445	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1446	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1447	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1448	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1449	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1450	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1451	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1452	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1453	
1454	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1455	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1456	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1457	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1458	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1459	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1460	
1461	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1462	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1463	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1464	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1465	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1466	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1467	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1468	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1469	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1470	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1471	
1472	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1473	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1474	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1475	  \[
1476	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1477	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1478	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1479	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1480	  \]
1481	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1482	\end{proof}
1483	
1484	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1485	
1486	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1487	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1488	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1489	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1490	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1491	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1492	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1493	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1494	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1495	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1496	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1497	
1498	\begin{definition}\leanok
1499	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1500	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1502	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1503	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1504	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1505	  ring homomorphism}
1506	  \[
1507	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1508	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1509	  \]
1510	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1511	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1512	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1513	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1514	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1515	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1516	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1517	\end{definition}
1518	
1519	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1520	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1521	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1523	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1524	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1525	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1526	  \(t_i\).
1527	\end{lemma}
1528	
1529	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1530	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1531	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1533	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1534	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1535	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1536	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1537	\end{lemma}
1538	
1539	\begin{definition}\leanok
1540	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1541	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1543	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1544	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1545	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1546	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1547	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1548	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1549	  is measured.
1550	\end{definition}
1551	
1552	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1553	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1554	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1556	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1557	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1558	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1559	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1560	\end{lemma}
1561	
1562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1563	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1564	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1565	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1566	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1567	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1568	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1569	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1570	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1571	\end{lemma}
1572	
1573	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1574	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1575	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1576	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1577	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1578	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1579	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1580	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1581	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1582	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1583	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1584	\end{lemma}
1585	
1586	\begin{definition}\leanok
1587	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1588	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1589	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1590	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1591	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1592	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1593	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1594	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1595	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1596	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1597	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1598	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1599	\end{definition}
1600	
1601	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1602	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1603	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1604	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1605	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1606	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1607	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1608	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1609	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1610	\end{lemma}

1612	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1613	
1614	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1615	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1617	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1618	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib,
1619	    def:graded_polyEndHom, def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1620	    lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1621	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1622	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1623	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1624	  subquotients
1625	  \[
1626	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1627	  \]
1628	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1629	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1630	  \(r-1\), satisfying the finiteness condition. This is what is needed for the
1631	  inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1632	
1633	  The kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1634	  satisfy the same conditions as the original datum -- a homogeneous pair, pairwise-commuting
1635	  degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, a polynomial-ring module structure, and
1636	  the finiteness condition -- but of length \(r-1\) rather than \(r\).
1637	\end{lemma}
1638	
1639	\begin{proof}
1640	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1641	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib,
1642	    def:graded_polyEndHom, def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1643	    lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1644	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1645	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1646	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1647	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1648	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1649	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1650	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1651	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1652	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1653	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1654	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1655	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\).
1656	
1657	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1658	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1659	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1660	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1661	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1662	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1663	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1664	  polynomial rings
1665	  \[
1666	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1667	  \]
1668	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1669	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1670	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1671	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1672	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1673	
1674	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1675	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1676	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1677	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1678	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1679	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1680	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1681	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients.
1682	\end{proof}
1683	
1684	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1685	
1686	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1687	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1688	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1690	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1691	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1692	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1693	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1694	  \[
1695	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1696	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1697	  \]
1698	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1699	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1700	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1701	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1702	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1703	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1704	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1705	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1706	  \[
1707	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1708	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1709	  \]
1710	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1711	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1712	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1713	\end{lemma}
1714	
1715	\begin{proof}
1716	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1717	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1718	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1719	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1720	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1721	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1722	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1723	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1724	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1725	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1726	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1727	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1728	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1729	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1730	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1731	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1732	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1733	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1734	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1735	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1736	\end{proof}

1738	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
1739	
1740	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1741	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
1742	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1743	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1744	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
1745	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
1746	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
1747	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
1748	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
1749	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
1750	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
1751	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
1752	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
1753	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
1754	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
1755	\end{lemma}
1756	
1757	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1758	
1759	\begin{lemma}[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1760	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1762	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1763	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1764	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1765	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
1766	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1767	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1768	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1769	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1771	  degree-one generators).}
1772	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1773	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1774	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1775	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1776	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1777	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1778	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1779	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1780	  \(M\).
1781	\end{lemma}
1782	
1783	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1784	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1785	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1786	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1787	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1788	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1789	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1790	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1791	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1792	\begin{proof}
1793	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1794	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1795	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1796	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
1797	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1798	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1799	
1800	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1801	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1802	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1803	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1804	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1805	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1806	
1807	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1808	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1809	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1810	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1811	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1812	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1813	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1814	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1815	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1816	  \[
1817	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1818	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1819	  \]
1820	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
1821	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1822	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1823	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1824	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1825	\end{proof}

1827	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1828	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1829	
1830	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1831	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1832	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1833	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1834	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1835	
1836	\begin{definition}
1837	
1838	\leanok
1839	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1840	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1841	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1842	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1843	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1844	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1845	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1846	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1847	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1848	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1849	  % Polynomials" (read from
1850	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1851	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1852	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1853	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1854	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1855	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1856	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1857	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1858	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1859	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1860	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1861	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1862	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1863	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1864	  \[
1865	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1866	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1867	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1868	  \]
1869	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1870	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1871	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1872	
1873	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1874	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1875	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1876	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1877	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1878	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1879	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1880	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1881	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1882	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1883	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1884	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1885	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1886	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1887	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1888	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1889	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1890	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1891	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1892	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1893	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1894	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1895	
1896	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1897	\end{definition}

1902	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}

1912	  \]

1914	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence

1981	\begin{proof}
1982	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value,
1983	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor,
1984	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp,
1985	        def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
1986	  The concrete identity is the instantiation of the abstract variable-legs reindex
1987	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs} at the literal pullback projection legs
1988	  \(g' = \operatorname{pr}_1\), \(f' = \operatorname{pr}_2\): with the codomain read
1989	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} being definitionally the variable-legs read at
1990	  those legs, the concrete statement follows directly. The remainder of this proof records the
1991	  mathematical content of that abstract identity.
1992	
1993	  Throughout, let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the
1994	  canonical isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}, and
1995	  \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\), \(\iota_{R'} : R' \to A \otimes_R R'\) the tensor
1996	  inclusions, so that by Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} the two projection legs
1997	  factor as
1998	  \[
1999	    g' \;=\; e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A,
2000	    \qquad
2001	    f' \;=\; e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'} .
2002	  \]
2003	  The inner composite \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) is the four-factor map
2004	  \[
2005	    f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr) \;\circ\;
2006	    (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g', f})^{\mathrm{hom}} \;\circ\;
2007	    (\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}} \;\circ\;
2008	    (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f', g})^{\mathrm{inv}},
2009	  \]
2010	  with \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\), \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\),
2011	  \(\eta^{g'}\) the unit of the \((g')\)-adjunction, and
2012	  \(w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) the commutativity of the square.
2013	
2014	  \emph{Why a direct substitution fails.} The identity cannot be obtained by substituting the
2015	  leg factorizations directly, because the legs \(g'\) and \(f'\) appear in multiple positions
2016	  simultaneously: as the index of the adjunction \(\eta^{g'}\), inside the equality proof
2017	  \(w\) parametrizing \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w\), inside the argument
2018	  \(f'_{*}\bigl((g')^{*}\widetilde M\bigr)\) of the codomain comparison
2019	  \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), and inside the opaque codomain
2020	  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} = \) Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
2021	  The reindex is therefore restructured into three sub-steps so that the legs become explicit
2022	  free variables; it is \emph{not} a single rewrite.
2023	
2024	  \emph{(i) Abstract variable-legs restatement.} Restate the codomain read — and, with it, the
2025	  whole four-factor chain — for \emph{generic} morphisms \(g', f'\) carrying the cone-leg
2026	  equalities \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) and
2027	  \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\) as explicit hypotheses, in place of the literal
2028	  projections \(\operatorname{pr}_1, \operatorname{pr}_2\). In this abstract form the legs are
2029	  free variables, not terms pinned by the pullback's universal property, so each can be
2030	  eliminated by substituting its defining equation: the equality proof \(w\), the adjunction
2031	  index, and the \(\gamma_{\psi}\)/\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) arguments are all expressed in the
2032	  free leg variables and transform uniformly under the substitution. This restatement converts
2033	  the blocked rewrite into a legitimate one; it is the structural heart of the step.
2034	
2035	  \emph{(ii) \(\Gamma\)-collapse of the transparent coherences.} On global sections, read through
2036	  the \(\Gamma\)-pushforward dictionaries (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso},
2037	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), the two pushforward-composition factors and the
2038	  congruence factor are transparent. The composition coherences collapse to the identity —
2039	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) and
2040	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f',g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) evaluate, on the top open, to the identity
2041	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2042	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2043	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2044	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2045	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
2046	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
2047	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2048	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2049	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2050	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2051	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2052	  sections.
2053	
2054	  \emph{(iii) Reduction to Seam~1 via the leg-reindex engine.} With the legs substituted
2055	  (step~(i)) to \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), the leg-reindex engine
2056	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
2057	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), rewrites the unit factor
2058	  \(\eta^{g'} = \eta^{e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\), together with the
2059	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})\) coherence, into the affine
2060	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit followed by \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\) of the
2061	  \(e\)-unit. Since \(e\) is an isomorphism of schemes, its pullback functor is an equivalence
2062	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}) and the \(e\)-unit is invertible, hence an
2063	  isomorphism absorbed into the codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). The surviving
2064	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit has section value over \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) equal to
2065	  the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Seam~1
2066	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
2067	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2068	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) and reading over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by
2069	  restriction of scalars along \(\psi\) identifies all four factors with the restriction of
2070	  scalars along \(\psi\) of the codomain-read transport of the unit value, namely
2071	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\)
2072	  (Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}) — the
2073	  ``\(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), \(\mathcal F'\) is
2074	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)'' bookkeeping of the source. Each of (i)--(iii) extracts as a
2075	  named lemma; together they close the reindex without ever rewriting the bare legs.
2076	\end{proof}
