2133	\begin{proof}
2134	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
2135	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv,
2136	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso,
2137	        lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
2138	  Seam~3 is the pullback-side companion of Seam~2: where Seam~2 reads the inner composite
2139	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), here we read its
2140	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose over \(\operatorname{Spec} R'\), and the result is the
2141	  extension of scalars along \(\psi\) of the Seam~2 value \(\rho\).
2142	
2143	  \emph{Counit factorization.} The base-change map
2144	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the adjunction transpose of
2145	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\) with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\). By the
2146	  counit form of the hom-set adjunction (\(\operatorname{homEquiv}\)-counit identity for the
2147	  transpose), it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the counit
2148	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining:
2149	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).
2150	
2151	  \emph{The concrete counit coherence — why it is not a bare computation.} Conjugating
2152	  \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by \(\varepsilon_g\) by the domain identification
2153	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and the codomain
2154	  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read})
2155	  requires the pullback dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\) of
2156	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} — which reads \(g^* = (\operatorname{Spec}\psi)^*\) of a
2157	  tilde as extension of scalars along \(\psi\) — to interact with the geometric counit
2158	  \(\varepsilon_g\). This interaction is \emph{not} a definitional reduction: \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is
2159	  built abstractly as the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints comparison, evaluated from the
2160	  natural-isomorphism of right adjoints \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso})
2161	  via the conjugate-of-isomorphisms equivalence; concretely \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is the component at the
2162	  module of
2163	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{conjIso}(\mathrm{adj}_L, \mathrm{adj}_R)^{-1}(\gamma_{\psi})\bigr)^{-1}\),
2164	  where \(\mathrm{adj}_L = (\widetilde{(-)}_R \dashv \Gamma_R) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\) is the
2165	  composite ``tilde then pullback'' adjunction and
2166	  \(\mathrm{adj}_R = (\,{}^{\psi}\!(-) \dashv {}_{\psi}(-)) . (\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv
2167	  \Gamma_{R'})\) is the composite ``extension of scalars then tilde'' adjunction. Because the
2168	  dictionary is opaque in this way, the counit cannot be simplified against it by reflexivity
2169	  or by definitional unfolding; the identification must be made through the abstract conjugate calculus.
2170	
2171	  \emph{Conjugation via the composed-adjunction counit-triangle identity.} The required identity
2172	  is the counit-triangle (zig-zag) coherence for the \emph{composed} adjunction
2173	  \((\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv \Gamma_{R'}) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\), the same composite adjunction
2174	  whose left adjoint \(\Pi_{\psi}\) compares. Read concretely, this coherence states that the
2175	  dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\), composed with the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\) and the
2176	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit, equals the algebraic counit of the
2177	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction \(({}^{\psi}\!(-) \dashv {}_{\psi}(-))\) along
2178	  \(\psi\); equivalently, conjugating \(\Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\) by the tilde dictionaries
2179	  identifies it with the module-level adjunction counit of
2180	  the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\). This is the dual companion of the
2181	  unit-across-conjugate coherence (Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}) used in Seam~1 and
2182	  Seam~2. Applying it, conjugation replaces the transpose by extension of scalars along \(\psi\)
2183	  applied to the \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading \(\rho\) of \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\)
2184	  from Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex} (Seam~2): the geometric counit collapses to
2185	  the module-level counit, and the residual \(g^*\)-leg becomes \({}^{\psi}\!(-) = \) extension of
2186	  scalars along \(\psi\) applied to \(\rho\).
2187	
2188	  \emph{Identification on generators.} Since \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), its
2189	  extension of scalars along \(\psi\) sends
2190	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). This
2191	  is precisely the inverse of the regrouping isomorphism
2192	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on generators, and both maps are
2193	  \(R'\)-linear, so they coincide.
2194	\end{proof}

2584	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}

2587	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
2588	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.

2590	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2591	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2592	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2593	  \mathlibok
2594	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2595	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2596	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2597	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2598	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2599	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2600	  \[
2601	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2602	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2603	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2604	  \]
2605	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2606	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2607	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2608	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2609	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2610	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2611	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2612	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2613	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2614	\end{lemma}

2648	  Consider the cartesian square
2649	  \[
2650	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2651	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
2652	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
2653	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
2654	    \end{array}
2655	  \]
2656	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
2657	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
2658	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
2659	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
2660	  \[
2661	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2662	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
2663	  \]

2665	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
2666	  \[

2668	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
2669	  \]
2670	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
2671	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
2672	  \(\mathcal{F}\).

2720	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain

2723	% $$

2725	% $$
2726	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
2727	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2728	% $$

2730	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.

2732	%

2734	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
2735	% {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
2736	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.
2737	% The reader who wishes to avoid this spectral sequence
2738	% can use Mayer-Vietoris and induction on $t$ as in the proof of
2739	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}."
2740	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
2741	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
2742	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
2743	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
2744	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
2745	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
2746	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
2747	\begin{proof}
2748	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2749	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
2750	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
2751	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
2752	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
2753	  sequence.
2754	
2755	  \medskip
2756	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
2757	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
2758	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
2759	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
2760	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
2761	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
2762	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
2763	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
2764	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
2765	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
2766	  \[
2767	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
2768	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
2769	  \]
2770	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
2771	
2772	  \medskip
2773	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
2774	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
2775	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
2776	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
2777	  \[
2778	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2779	    \;=\;
2780	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
2781	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
2782	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
2783	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
2784	    \Bigr),
2785	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
2786	  \]
2787	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
2788	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
2789	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2790	  higher cohomology.
2791	
2792	  \medskip
2793	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2794	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2795	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2796	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2797	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2798	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2799	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2800	  \[
2801	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2802	    \qquad
2803	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2804	  \]
2805	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2806	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2807	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2808	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2809	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
2810	  \[
2811	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2812	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2813	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2814	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2815	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2816	  \]
2817	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2818	
2819	  \medskip
2820	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2821	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2822	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2823	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2824	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2825	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2826	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2827	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2828	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2829	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2830	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2831	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2832	  \[
2833	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2834	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2835	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2836	  \]
2837	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2838	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2839	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2840	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2841	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2842	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2843	\end{proof}

1920	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1921	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1922	  localization]
1923	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1924	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1925	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1926	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1927	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1928	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1929	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1930	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1931	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1932	  \[
1933	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1934	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1935	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1936	  \]
1937	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1938	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1939	
1940	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1941	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1942	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1943	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1944	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1945	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1946	\end{lemma}
1947	
1948	\begin{proof}
1949	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1950	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1951	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1952	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1953	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1954	
1955	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1956	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1957	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1958	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1959	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1960	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1961	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1962	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1963	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1964	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1965	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1966	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1967	\end{proof}

1969	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
1970	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1971	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
1972	  \mathlibok
1973	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1974	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
1975	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
1976	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
1977	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
1978	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
1979	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
1980	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
1981	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
1982	\end{lemma}

1984	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
1985	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1986	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
1987	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1988	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
1989	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
1990	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
1991	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1992	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
1993	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1994	  %  associated to $M$.
1995	  %  [...]
1996	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
1997	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
1998	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
1999	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2000	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2001	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2002	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2003	  \begin{enumerate}
2004	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2005	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2006	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2007	    \item the section-restriction map
2008	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2009	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2010	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2011	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2012	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2013	  \end{enumerate}
2014	\end{lemma}
2015	
2016	\begin{proof}
2017	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2018	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2019	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2020	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2021	
2022	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2023	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2024	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2025	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2026	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2027	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2028	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2029	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2030	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2031	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2032	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2033	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2034	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2035	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2036	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2037	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2038	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2039	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2040	\end{proof}

2062	  \[

2064	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
2065	  \]

2067	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
2068	  \(X\).

2071	\begin{definition}\leanok
2072	[Schematic-support immersion]
2073	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
2074	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
2075	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
2076	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2077	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2078	  \[
2079	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
2080	  \]
2081	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
2082	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
2083	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
2084	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
2085	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
2086	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
2087	\end{definition}

2089	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2090	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2091	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2092	  \mathlibok
2093	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2094	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2095	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2096	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2097	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2098	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2099	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2100	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2101	\end{lemma}

2108	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
2109	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
2110	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
2111	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
2112	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
2113	  % + L418-L419).
2114	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2115	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
2116	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
2117	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
2118	  %  [...]
2119	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2120	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
2121	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2122	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
2123	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
2124	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
2125	  \[
2126	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
2127	  \]

2129	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back

2131	  functor's pullback action requires.

2163	\begin{definition}\leanok
2164	[Quot functor]
2165	  \label{def:quot_functor}
2166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
2167	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
2168	
2169	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
2170	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
2171	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
2172	  % + L481-L498).
2173	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2174	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
2175	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
2176	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
2177	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
2178	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
2179	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
2180	  %
2181	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
2182	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
2183	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
2184	  %
2185	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
2186	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
2187	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
2188	  %
2189	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
2190	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
2191	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
2192	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
2193	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
2194	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
2195	  %  well-defined, which gives
2196	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
2197	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
2198	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
2199	  %  [...]
2200	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
2201	  %  as a co-product
2202	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
2203	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
2204	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
2205	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
2206	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
2207	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
2208	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
2209	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2210	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2211	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
2212	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
2213	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
2214	  \[
2215	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
2216	  \]
2217	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
2218	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
2219	  \begin{enumerate}
2220	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
2221	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
2222	      which is flat over \(T\);
2223	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
2224	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
2225	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
2226	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
2227	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
2228	      \(\Phi\).
2229	  \end{enumerate}
2230	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
2231	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
2232	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
2233	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
2234	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
2235	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
2236	\end{definition}

2238	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
2239	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
2240	
2241	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
2242	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
2243	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
2244	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
2245	
2246	\begin{definition}\leanok
2247	[Grassmannian scheme]
2248	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
2249	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
2250	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
2251	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
2252	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
2253	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
2254	  % + L773-L850).
2255	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2256	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
2257	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
2258	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
2259	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
2260	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
2261	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
2262	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
2263	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
2264	  %  contravariant functor
2265	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
2266	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
2267	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2268	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
2269	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
2270	  %  [...]
2271	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
2272	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
2273	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
2274	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
2275	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
2276	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
2277	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
2278	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
2279	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
2280	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
2281	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
2282	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2283	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
2284	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
2285	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
2286	  \[
2287	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
2288	    \qquad
2289	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
2290	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
2291	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
2292	  \]
2293	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
2294	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
2295	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
2296	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
2297	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
2298	\end{definition}

2382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
2383	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
2384	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
2385	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
2386	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
2387	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
2388	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
2389	\begin{proof}
2390	  
2391	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
2392	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
2393	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
2394	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
2395	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
2396	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
2397	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
2398	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
2399	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
2400	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
2401	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
2402	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
2403	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
2404	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
2405	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
2406	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
2407	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
2408	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
2409	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
2410	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
2411	
2412	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
2413	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
2414	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
2415	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
2416	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
2417	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
2418	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
2419	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
2420	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
2421	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
2422	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
2423	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
2424	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
2425	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
2426	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
2427	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
2428	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
2429	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
2430	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
2431	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
2432	\end{proof}

368	\begin{proof}
369	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
370	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
371	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
372	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
373	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
374	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
375	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
376	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
377	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
378	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
379	\end{proof}

425	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
426	  normalisation with denominators).}
427	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
428	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
429	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
430	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
431	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
432	  \[
433	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
434	  \]
435	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
436	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
437	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
438	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
439	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
440	\end{lemma}

442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
444	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
445	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
446	  % NOTE (iter-007 decomposition): the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a
447	  % Finset-fold of lem:gf_clear_one_denominator over the finite generating set. Each
448	  % generator satisfies one equation of integral dependence whose coefficient
449	  % polynomials lie in K[b_1,...,b_n]; gf_clear_one_denominator clears the denominators
450	  % of one such coefficient polynomial into a single Localization.Away g_i. Folding over
451	  % the finite set of (generator, coefficient) pairs and multiplying the finitely many
452	  % g_i yields one common g != 0 over which all integrality relations hold; the AlgHom
453	  % assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
454	
455	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
456	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
457	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
458	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
459	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
460	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
461	
462	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
463	  finite set of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); each
464	  generator satisfies an equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are
465	  polynomials in \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\), and there are finitely many such
466	  coefficient polynomials in total. Applying \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} to each
467	  coefficient polynomial yields a non-zero element of \(A\) over which that polynomial's
468	  coefficients become integral; folding over the finite collection (multiplying the
469	  finitely many elements) produces a single \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that
470	  \emph{every} coefficient of \emph{every} integral-dependence equation lies in
471	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). After inverting \(g\) all these integrality relations hold
472	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the chosen generators generate \(B_g\) as a module
473	  over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
474	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
475	
476	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
477	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
478	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
479	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
480	
481	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
482	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
483	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
484	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
485	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
486	  \[
487	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
488	  \]
489	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
490	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
491	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
492	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
493	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
494	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
495	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
496	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
497	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
498	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
499	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
500	
501	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
502	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
503	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
504	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
505	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
506	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
507	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
508	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
509	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
510	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
511	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
512	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
513	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
514	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
515	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
516	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
517	
518	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
519	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
520	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
521	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
522	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
523	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
524	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
525	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
526	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
527	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
528	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
529	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
530	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
531	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
532	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
533	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
534	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
535	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
536	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
537	\end{proof}
